34.23: 'Won't all their livestock, their property, and their animals eventually become ours? Let us just agree to their terms, and they will settle here with us.'
34.15: 'We will agree to this on one condition only: all your males must be circumcised, becoming as we are.'
34.25: Three days later, when all the men were still in agony from the surgery, Simeon and Levi—two of Jacob's sons, full brothers to Dinah—drew their swords and slaughtered every male in the city without warning.
34.16: 'Then we will give you our daughters and take yours in marriage. We will settle among you and become one united people.'
34.14: 'We cannot do this,' they said. 'Giving our sister to an uncircumcised man would be a disgrace to us.'
34.27: Jacob's other sons came upon the dead men and plundered the city that had violated their sister.
34.31: But they answered, 'Should he have been allowed to treat our sister as a prostitute?'
34.10: 'Settle here among us. The land is open to you—live here, trade freely, and acquire property.'
34.12: 'Set the bride price as high as you wish. I will give whatever gift you demand. Only let me marry the young woman.'
34.30: Jacob said to Simeon and Levi, 'You have ruined me—you have made me repulsive to every Canaanite and Perizzite in this land. We are few in number. If they unite against me and attack, I and my whole family will be wiped out.'
34.20: Hamor and Shechem went to the gate of their city and addressed the men of the city:
34.8: But Hamor addressed them: 'My son Shechem is deeply in love with your daughter. Please give her to him as his wife.'
34.21: 'These men are peaceable toward us. Let them settle in the land and trade freely—there is plenty of room for them. We will marry their daughters and give our daughters to them.'
34.7: Meanwhile Jacob's sons had come in from the fields the moment they heard. They were furious and deeply outraged, because Shechem had done a vile and shameful thing against Israel by raping Jacob's daughter—something utterly unacceptable.
34.2: When Shechem the son of Hamor—the Hivite ruler of that area—saw her, he seized her, raped her, and violated her.
34.1: One day Dinah—the daughter Leah had borne to Jacob—went out to visit the women of the region.
34.29: They carried off all the wealth, the women and children, and stripped everything from the houses.
34.5: Jacob heard that his daughter Dinah had been violated. His sons were out in the fields with the livestock, so he said nothing until they returned.
34.26: They killed Hamor and his son Shechem with their swords and took Dinah from Shechem's house and left.
34.24: All the men of the city gate agreed with Hamor and Shechem, and every male in the city was circumcised.
34.18: This proposal satisfied Hamor and his son Shechem.
34.28: They seized the flocks, herds, donkeys, and everything else in the city and in the surrounding fields.
34.17: 'But if you refuse to be circumcised, we will take our sister and leave.'
34.9: 'Let us form marriage alliances with each other. Give us your daughters and take ours in return.'

35.18: As her life slipped away—for she was dying—she named her son Ben-oni, 'Son of My Sorrow.' But his father named him Benjamin—'Son of My Right Hand' or 'Son of Good Fortune.'
35.27: Jacob finally came home to his father Isaac at Mamre—Kiriath-arba, that is Hebron—where Abraham and Isaac had lived as sojourners.
35.22:
While Israel was living in that region, Reuben slept with his father's concubine Bilhah. Israel heard what had happened.

Jacob had twelve sons:
35.14: Jacob set up a stone pillar at the spot where God had spoken with him. He poured a drink offering over it and anointed it with oil.
35.12: 'The land I gave to Abraham and Isaac—I now give to you, and I will give it to your descendants after you.'
35.21: Israel traveled on and set up camp beyond the tower of Eder.
35.4: So everyone handed over to Jacob their foreign gods and the earrings they wore, and Jacob buried them under the great tree near Shechem.
35.7: There he built an altar and named the place El-bethel—'God of Bethel'—because it was there that God had revealed himself to him when he was running from Esau.
35.1: God said to Jacob, 'Get up and go to Bethel and make your home there. Build an altar there to the God who appeared to you when you were fleeing from your brother Esau.'
35.5: As they traveled, a divinely sent terror fell on all the surrounding towns, and no one dared to pursue Jacob's sons.
35.3: 'Then we will go up to Bethel, where I will build an altar to the God who answered me when I was in trouble and who has been with me every step of the way.'
35.11: God said to him, 'I am God Almighty. Be fruitful and multiply. A great nation—indeed, a whole community of nations—will spring from you, and kings will descend from your own body.'
35.10: God said to him, 'Your name is Jacob—but from now on you will be called Israel.' So God named him Israel.
35.13: Then God departed from him at the place where he had spoken.
35.9: God appeared to Jacob once more—this time after his return from Paddan-aram—and blessed him.
35.2: So Jacob said to his household and to all who were with him, 'Get rid of every foreign god you have. Purify yourselves and put on fresh clothes.'
35.15: Jacob named the place where God had spoken with him Bethel—'House of God.'
35.8: It was there that Deborah, Rebekah's nurse, died. She was buried under the oak below Bethel, and it was called Allon-bacuth—'Oak of Weeping.'
35.17: When her labor was at its most severe, the midwife said to her, 'Don't be afraid—you have another son!'
35.29: Then he breathed his last and died, joining his ancestors. He was old and had lived a full life. His sons Esau and Jacob buried him together.
35.16: They traveled on from Bethel. While they were still some distance from Ephrath, Rachel went into labor—and it was a very difficult labor.
35.19: So Rachel died and was buried beside the road to Ephrath—that is, Bethlehem.
35.6: Jacob and all his company arrived at Luz—that is, Bethel—in the land of Canaan.
35.26: The sons of Leah's servant Zilpah: Gad and Asher. These were the twelve sons of Jacob born to him in Paddan-aram.
35.23: The sons of Leah: Reuben (Jacob's firstborn), Simeon, Levi, Judah, Issachar, and Zebulun.
35.25: The sons of Rachel's servant Bilhah: Dan and Naphtali.
35.24: The sons of Rachel: Joseph and Benjamin.
35.28: Isaac lived to be 180 years old.
35.20: Jacob set up a pillar over her grave. It stands there to this day as the Pillar of Rachel's Tomb.

36.24: Zibeon's sons: Aiah and Anah. This is the Anah who discovered the hot springs in the desert while tending his father Zibeon's donkeys.
36.43: Chief Magdiel and Chief Iram. These were the chiefs of Edom—that is, Esau, father of the Edomites—listed according to their settlements in the land they occupied.
36.9: This is the family line of Esau, ancestor of the Edomites in the hill country of Seir.
36.32: Bela the son of Beor reigned in Edom, with his capital at Dinhabah.
36.8: So Esau—that is, Edom—settled in the hill country of Seir.
36.16: Chief Korah, Chief Gatam, and Chief Amalek. These were the chiefs descended from Eliphaz in the land of Edom—grandsons through Adah.
36.17: From Reuel, Esau's son: Chief Nahath, Chief Zerah, Chief Shammah, and Chief Mizzah. These were the chiefs descended from Reuel in the land of Edom—grandsons through Esau's wife Basemath.
36.21: Dishon, Ezer, and Dishan. These were the Horite chiefs, descendants of Seir in the land of Edom.
36.31: These are the kings who ruled in the land of Edom before any king reigned over Israel:
36.1: This is the family record of Esau—the man also called Edom.
36.19: These were the sons of Esau—that is, Edom—and these were their chiefs.
36.41: Chief Oholibamah, Chief Elah, Chief Pinon,
36.5: and Oholibamah bore Jeush, Jalam, and Korah. These were the sons born to Esau in the land of Canaan.
36.2: Esau had taken his wives from among the Canaanite women: Adah the daughter of Elon the Hittite; Oholibamah the daughter of Anah, granddaughter of Zibeon the Hivite;
36.25: Anah's children: Dishon and Oholibamah the daughter of Anah.
36.14: The sons of Oholibamah—daughter of Anah, granddaughter of Zibeon, and wife of Esau—whom she bore to Esau: Jeush, Jalam, and Korah.
36.18: From Oholibamah, Esau's wife: Chief Jeush, Chief Jalam, and Chief Korah. These were the chiefs descended from Oholibamah daughter of Anah, Esau's wife.
36.11: Eliphaz's sons: Teman, Omar, Zepho, Gatam, and Kenaz.
36.15: These were the clan chiefs among Esau's descendants: From Eliphaz, Esau's firstborn: Chief Teman, Chief Omar, Chief Zepho, Chief Kenaz,
36.23: Shobal's sons: Alvan, Manahath, Ebal, Shepho, and Onam.
36.6: Esau took his wives, sons, daughters, and everyone in his household—along with his livestock and all the possessions he had accumulated in Canaan—and moved to a land far from his brother Jacob.
36.22: Lotan's sons: Hori and Hemam. Lotan's sister was Timna.
36.3: and Basemath, Ishmael's daughter, the sister of Nebaioth.
36.30: Chief Dishon, Chief Ezer, and Chief Dishan. These were the Horite chiefs, listed by division, in the land of Seir.
36.42: Chief Kenaz, Chief Teman, Chief Mibzar,
36.29: These were the Horite chiefs: Chief Lotan, Chief Shobal, Chief Zibeon, Chief Anah,
36.40: These were the chiefs descended from Esau, listed by name according to their clans and territories: Chief Timna, Chief Alvah, Chief Jetheth,
36.10: Esau's sons: Eliphaz, born to his wife Adah; and Reuel, born to his wife Basemath.
36.4: Adah bore Eliphaz to Esau; Basemath bore Reuel;
36.12: (Eliphaz also had a concubine named Timna, who bore him Amalek—ancestor of the Amalekites.) These were the grandsons through Esau's wife Adah.
36.27: Ezer's sons: Bilhan, Zaavan, and Akan.
36.7: Their combined possessions were simply too great to remain in the same place. The land could not support both of them because of their enormous flocks and herds.
36.34: When Jobab died, Husham from the land of the Temanites succeeded him.
36.20: These were the sons of Seir the Horite, the original inhabitants of the land: Lotan, Shobal, Zibeon, Anah,
36.26: Dishon's sons: Hemdan, Eshban, Ithran, and Cheran.
36.39: When Baal-hanan died, Hadar succeeded him. His capital was Pau; his wife's name was Mehetabel, the daughter of Matred and granddaughter of Mezahab.
36.13: Reuel's sons: Nahath, Zerah, Shammah, and Mizzah. These were the grandsons through Esau's wife Basemath.
36.35: When Husham died, Hadad the son of Bedad—who had defeated Midian in the territory of Moab—succeeded him. His capital was Avith.
36.33: When Bela died, Jobab the son of Zerah from Bozrah succeeded him.
36.38: When Shaul died, Baal-hanan the son of Achbor succeeded him.
36.36: When Hadad died, Samlah from Masrekah succeeded him.
36.37: When Samlah died, Shaul from Rehoboth-on-the-River succeeded him.

37.1: Jacob settled in the land of Canaan, where his father had lived as a foreigner.
37.2: This is the story of Jacob's family. Joseph was seventeen years old and worked as a shepherd alongside his brothers—the sons of Bilhah and Zilpah, his father's wives. He brought his father bad reports about them.
37.3: Now Israel loved Joseph more than any of his other sons, because Joseph had been born to him in his old age. He gave him a richly ornamented robe.
37.4: When his brothers saw that their father loved Joseph more than all of them, they hated Joseph so deeply that they could not speak a civil word to him.
37.5: Joseph had a dream, and when he told his brothers about it, their hatred for him grew even deeper.
37.6: He said, 'Listen to what I dreamed:'
37.7: 'We were out in the field binding bundles of grain. Suddenly my bundle stood up straight, and all your bundles gathered around it and bowed down to mine.'
37.8: His brothers shot back, 'So you think you're going to rule over us? You think you'll be our king?' They hated him even more because of his dreams and the things he said.
37.9: He had another dream and told his brothers about it. 'I had another dream,' he said. 'This time the sun, the moon, and eleven stars all bowed down to me.'
37.10: When he told his father and his brothers, his father scolded him. 'What kind of dream is that?' he said. 'Do you really think your mother and I and your brothers will come and bow down before you?'
37.11: His brothers were filled with jealousy. But his father kept thinking about it.
37.12: Some time later, his brothers went to graze their father's flocks near Shechem.
37.13: Israel said to Joseph, 'Your brothers are grazing the flocks near Shechem. Come, I'll send you to them.' Joseph replied, 'I'm ready.'
37.14: His father told him, 'Go and see how your brothers and the flocks are doing, and come back and tell me.' He sent Joseph from the Valley of Hebron. When Joseph arrived at Shechem,
37.15: a man found him wandering in the open country. 'What are you looking for?' the man asked.
37.16: Joseph said, 'I'm trying to find my brothers. Can you tell me where they are grazing their flocks?'
37.17: 'They've moved on,' the man said. 'I heard them say they were going to Dothan.' So Joseph went after his brothers and found them at Dothan.
37.18: They spotted him in the distance, and before he even reached them they were already plotting to kill him.
37.19: They said to each other, 'Here comes the dreamer!'
37.20: 'Let's kill him and throw him into one of these cisterns. We can say a wild animal ate him. Then we'll see what becomes of his precious dreams!'
37.21: But Reuben heard the plan and tried to save Joseph. 'Let's not kill him,' he said.
37.22: 'Don't shed his blood,' Reuben said. 'Throw him into this cistern out here in the wilderness, but don't lay a hand on him.' He planned to come back later and rescue Joseph and return him to their father.
37.23: When Joseph arrived, his brothers tore off his ornate robe
37.24: and threw him into a cistern. It was empty—no water, just a dry pit.
37.25: Then they sat down to eat. Looking up, they saw a caravan of Ishmaelite traders approaching from Gilead, their camels loaded with spices, balm, and myrrh, bound for Egypt.
37.26: Judah said to his brothers, 'What do we gain by killing our brother and hiding his blood?'
37.27: 'Let's sell him to the Ishmaelites instead. That way we won't have blood on our hands—he is our brother, our own flesh and blood.' His brothers agreed.
37.28: So when the Midianite traders came by, his brothers hauled Joseph up from the cistern and sold him to the Ishmaelites for twenty pieces of silver. The traders took Joseph to Egypt.
37.29: When Reuben came back to the cistern and found Joseph gone, he tore his clothes in grief.
37.30: He went back to his brothers and cried, 'The boy is gone! What am I going to do?'
37.31: They took Joseph's robe, slaughtered a goat, and dipped the robe in the blood.
37.32: They brought the robe to their father and said, 'We found this. Is it your son's robe?'
37.33: Jacob recognized it immediately. 'It is my son's robe!' he cried. 'A wild animal has devoured him—Joseph has been torn to pieces!'
37.34: Jacob tore his own clothes, put on sackcloth, and mourned for his son for many days.
37.35: All his sons and daughters came to comfort him, but he refused to be consoled. 'No,' he said, 'I will go to my grave mourning for my son.' And his father wept bitterly for him.
37.36: Meanwhile, the Midianites had sold Joseph in Egypt to a man named Potiphar, one of Pharaoh's officials—the captain of the guard.

38.1: Around that time, Judah left his brothers and settled with a man from Adullam named Hirah.
38.2: There Judah met and married the daughter of a Canaanite man named Shua.
38.3: She became pregnant and gave birth to a son, whom Judah named Er.
38.4: She conceived again and gave birth to a son she named Onan.
38.5: She had a third son and named him Shelah. Judah was at Chezib when Shelah was born.
38.6: Judah found a wife for his firstborn Er. Her name was Tamar.
38.7: But Er was wicked in the LORD's eyes, and the LORD put him to death.
38.8: Judah said to Onan, 'Sleep with your brother's wife and fulfill your duty as her brother-in-law, to carry on your brother's line.'
38.9: But Onan knew that any child born would not be counted as his heir. So every time he slept with his brother's wife, he prevented conception, refusing to give his brother a descendant.
38.10: What Onan did was evil in the LORD's eyes, so the LORD put him to death as well.
38.11: Judah said to his daughter-in-law Tamar, 'Go back to your father's house and remain a widow until my son Shelah is old enough to marry.' He was afraid that Shelah would die like his brothers. So Tamar went to live in her father's house.
38.12: After some time, Judah's wife—the daughter of Shua—died. When Judah had finished his mourning, he went up to Timnah to oversee the shearing of his sheep, accompanied by his friend Hirah the Adullamite.
38.13: When Tamar learned that her father-in-law was going up to Timnah to shear his sheep,
38.14: she took off her widow's clothes, put on a veil to disguise herself, and sat down at the entrance to Enaim along the road to Timnah. She had realized that Shelah was now grown up but she had not been given to him as his wife.
38.15: When Judah saw her, he thought she was a prostitute, because she had covered her face.
38.16: Not knowing she was his daughter-in-law, he approached her and said, 'Come, let me sleep with you.' She asked, 'What will you give me?'
38.17: 'I'll send you a goat from my flock,' he said. She replied, 'Only if you give me a pledge until it arrives.'
38.18: 'What kind of pledge?' he asked. 'Your seal with its cord, and the staff you're carrying,' she said. He gave them to her and slept with her, and she became pregnant by him.
38.19: After he left, she removed her veil and put her widow's clothes back on.
38.20: Judah sent his Adullamite friend back with the young goat to reclaim his pledge, but the man could not find the woman.
38.21: He asked the men of that place, 'Where is the woman who was sitting by the road at Enaim?' They said, 'There has been no such woman here.'
38.22: He returned to Judah and said, 'I couldn't find her. The men of the place even told me there was no such woman there.'
38.23: Judah said, 'Then let her keep what she has. We don't want to be made a laughingstock. I sent the goat as agreed, but you couldn't find her.'
38.24: About three months later Judah received this report: 'Your daughter-in-law Tamar has acted like a prostitute—in fact, she is pregnant.' Judah said, 'Bring her out and have her burned!'
38.25: As they were bringing her out, she sent a message to her father-in-law: 'I am pregnant by the man who owns these things.' She added, 'Do you recognize this seal and cord and staff?'
38.26: Judah recognized them and said, 'She is more in the right than I am, because I withheld my son Shelah from her.' He did not sleep with her again.
38.27: When it was time for her to give birth, there were twins in her womb.
38.28: During the birth, one of them reached out his hand, and the midwife tied a scarlet thread on his wrist, saying, 'This one came out first.'
38.29: But he pulled his hand back, and his brother came out instead. 'What a break you've made for yourself!' the midwife exclaimed. So he was named Perez—meaning 'breaking out.'
38.30: Then his brother came out with the scarlet thread on his wrist, and he was named Zerah.

39.1: Joseph had been taken down to Egypt, where Potiphar—one of Pharaoh's officials, the captain of the guard—had purchased him from the Ishmaelites.
39.2: The LORD was with Joseph, and he became a man of remarkable success. He lived in the household of his Egyptian master.
39.3: His master could see that the LORD was with him—that the LORD made everything Joseph did succeed.
39.4: Joseph won his master's trust and became his personal attendant. Potiphar put him in charge of his entire household and entrusted everything he owned to Joseph's care.
39.5: From the moment Potiphar put Joseph in charge, the LORD blessed the Egyptian's household for Joseph's sake. The LORD's blessing rested on everything Potiphar owned—his house, his fields, everything.
39.6: Potiphar handed everything over to Joseph's management and concerned himself with nothing except what he ate for dinner. Joseph was well-built and handsome.
39.7: After a while, his master's wife set her eyes on Joseph and said, 'Come to bed with me.'
39.8: But Joseph refused. 'My master has handed everything in this house over to me,' he said. 'He trusts me completely with everything he owns.'
39.9: 'He has not kept anything back from me except you—because you are his wife. How could I do something so wicked? I would be sinning against God.'
39.10: Though she pressed him day after day, he refused to sleep with her or even to be alone with her.
39.11: One day Joseph went into the house to do his work, and none of the other household servants were around.
39.12: She grabbed him by his cloak and said, 'Come to bed with me!' But Joseph left his cloak in her hand and ran out of the house.
39.13: When she realized he had left his cloak in her hand and fled,
39.14: she called the household servants and told them, 'This Hebrew slave was brought in here to make fools of us! He tried to sleep with me, and I screamed.'
39.15: 'When he heard me scream, he ran out, leaving his cloak right here.'
39.16: She kept the cloak beside her until Potiphar came home.
39.17: Then she told him the same story: 'That Hebrew slave you brought to us came in to make a mockery of me.'
39.18: 'When I screamed, he ran off and left his cloak right there.'
39.19: When Potiphar heard his wife's account—'This is what your slave did to me'—his anger blazed.
39.20: Potiphar had Joseph arrested and thrown into the prison where the king's prisoners were held. And there Joseph remained in prison.
39.21: But the LORD was with Joseph. He showed Joseph his faithful covenant loyalty and gave him favor in the eyes of the prison warden.
39.22: The warden put Joseph in charge of all the other prisoners and made him responsible for everything that happened in the prison.
39.23: The warden stopped paying close attention to anything under Joseph's care, because the LORD was with Joseph and made everything he did succeed.

40.1: Sometime later, the king of Egypt's cupbearer and his baker both offended their master the king.
40.2: Pharaoh was furious with his two officers—the chief cupbearer and the chief baker—
40.3: and had them imprisoned in the house of the captain of the guard—the same prison where Joseph was held.
40.4: The captain of the guard put Joseph in charge of them, and he served them. They remained in prison for some time.
40.5: One night both of them—the cupbearer and the baker of the king of Egypt, both held in the prison—had dreams. Each dream had its own distinct meaning.
40.6: When Joseph came to them the next morning, he could see they were upset.
40.7: He asked Pharaoh's officials, 'Why do you both look so troubled today?'
40.8: 'We both had dreams,' they said, 'but there is no one here to interpret them.' Joseph said, 'Interpretations belong to God. Tell me your dreams.'
40.9: The chief cupbearer told Joseph his dream: 'In my dream I saw a vine in front of me.'
40.10: 'The vine had three branches. As soon as it budded, it blossomed, and its clusters ripened into grapes.'
40.11: 'I was holding Pharaoh's cup. I took the grapes, squeezed them into Pharaoh's cup, and placed it in his hand.'
40.12: Joseph said to him, 'Here is its meaning: the three branches are three days.'
40.13: 'Within three days Pharaoh will release you and restore you to your position. You will place Pharaoh's cup in his hand, just as you used to do.'
40.14: 'When things go well for you, please remember me. Do me this favor—mention me to Pharaoh and help get me out of this place.'
40.15: 'I was kidnapped from the land of the Hebrews, and here I have done nothing to deserve being thrown into prison.'
40.16: When the chief baker saw that the interpretation had been favorable, he said to Joseph, 'I also had a dream. On my head were three baskets of bread.'
40.17: 'The top basket was full of baked goods for Pharaoh, but the birds kept eating them right out of the basket on my head.'
40.18: Joseph said, 'Here is its meaning: the three baskets are three days.'
40.19: 'Within three days Pharaoh will lift off your head—he will execute you and hang your body on a pole, and the birds will eat your flesh.'
40.20: The third day was Pharaoh's birthday, and he held a feast for all his servants. He made an announcement about the chief cupbearer and the chief baker in front of everyone.
40.21: He restored the chief cupbearer to his former position, and the cupbearer placed the cup in Pharaoh's hand once more.
40.22: But he had the chief baker executed, exactly as Joseph had interpreted.
40.23: The chief cupbearer, however, forgot all about Joseph.

41.1: Two full years passed. Then Pharaoh had a dream. He was standing by the Nile River
41.2: when seven sleek, fat cows came up out of the river and grazed among the reeds.
41.3: Then seven other cows came up after them—ugly and gaunt—and they stood beside the fat cows on the riverbank.
41.4: The ugly, gaunt cows devoured the seven sleek, fat cows. Then Pharaoh woke up.
41.5: He fell asleep again and had a second dream: Seven heads of grain—healthy and full—grew on a single stalk.
41.6: Then seven other heads of grain sprouted—thin and scorched by the east wind.
41.7: The thin heads of grain swallowed up the seven full, healthy heads. Pharaoh woke up and realized it had been a dream.
41.8: In the morning Pharaoh was deeply troubled. He summoned all the magicians and wise men of Egypt and told them his dreams, but no one could interpret them.
41.9: Then the chief cupbearer spoke up: 'I must confess my failure today.'
41.10: 'Pharaoh was once angry with his servants and had me and the chief baker imprisoned in the captain of the guard's house.'
41.11: 'One night we each had a dream, and each dream had its own meaning.'
41.12: 'There was a young Hebrew man there with us—a servant of the captain of the guard. We told him our dreams, and he interpreted each one correctly.'
41.13: 'And it happened exactly as he said—I was restored to my position, and the baker was executed.'
41.14: Pharaoh sent for Joseph at once. They hurried him out of the dungeon. He shaved, put on fresh clothes, and appeared before Pharaoh.
41.15: Pharaoh said to him, 'I had a dream, and no one can interpret it. But I have heard that when you hear a dream, you can interpret it.'
41.16: Joseph replied, 'I cannot do it myself—but God will give Pharaoh the answer that he needs.'
41.17: Pharaoh told Joseph his dream: 'In my dream I was standing on the bank of the Nile.'
41.18: 'Seven sleek, fat cows came up out of the river and grazed among the reeds.'
41.19: 'Then seven other cows came up after them—scrawny, very ugly, and thin. I had never seen such wretched cows in all of Egypt.'
41.20: 'The gaunt, ugly cows devoured the seven fat cows.'
41.21: 'But even after eating them, you could not tell they had done so—they looked just as gaunt as before. Then I woke up.'
41.22: 'Then I had another dream: seven heads of grain, full and good, were growing on a single stalk.'
41.23: 'Then seven other heads sprouted after them—withered, thin, and scorched by the east wind.'
41.24: 'The thin heads swallowed up the seven good heads. I told this to the magicians, but none of them could explain it.'
41.25: Joseph said to Pharaoh, 'Both dreams mean the same thing. God has shown Pharaoh what he is about to do.'
41.26: 'The seven fat cows and the seven full heads of grain both represent seven years—they are one and the same dream.'
41.27: 'The seven gaunt cows that came after them and the seven scorched, empty heads of grain both represent seven years of famine.'
41.28: 'This is what I said to Pharaoh: God has revealed what he is about to do.'
41.29: 'Seven years of great abundance are coming to the whole land of Egypt.'
41.30: 'After them will come seven years of terrible famine. All the abundance will be forgotten; the famine will devastate the land.'
41.31: 'The abundance will be completely forgotten when the famine arrives, because it will be so severe.'
41.32: 'The reason the dream came to Pharaoh twice is that God has firmly decided to do this, and he will make it happen very soon.'
41.33: 'Therefore, Pharaoh should find a wise and discerning man and put him in charge of the land of Egypt.'
41.34: 'Let Pharaoh appoint administrators over the land and collect one-fifth of all the harvest during the seven years of abundance.'
41.35: 'Let them collect all the food produced in those good years and store the grain under Pharaoh's authority, keeping it in the cities.'
41.36: 'This stored food will be a reserve for the country, to be used during the seven years of famine ahead—so that Egypt will survive and not be destroyed by the famine.'
41.37: Pharaoh and all his officials approved of this plan.
41.38: Pharaoh said to his officials, 'Can we find anyone else like this man—someone in whom the Spirit of God lives?'
41.39: Pharaoh said to Joseph, 'Since God has revealed all this to you, there is no one as discerning and wise as you.'
41.40: 'You will be in charge of my palace. All my people will obey your commands. Only in the matter of the throne will I rank above you.'
41.41: Pharaoh declared, 'I hereby place you in charge of the entire land of Egypt.'
41.42: Pharaoh removed his signet ring and placed it on Joseph's finger. He dressed him in robes of fine linen and put a gold chain around his neck.
41.43: He had Joseph ride in the chariot of his second-in-command, and men ran ahead crying, 'Bow the knee!' In this way Pharaoh set Joseph over all of Egypt.
41.44: Pharaoh said to Joseph, 'I am Pharaoh, but without your approval no one in all Egypt will make a move.'
41.45: Pharaoh gave Joseph the Egyptian name Zaphenath-paneah and gave him Asenath—daughter of Potiphera, priest of On—as his wife. Joseph then traveled throughout the land of Egypt.
41.46: Joseph was thirty years old when he entered the service of Pharaoh king of Egypt. He left Pharaoh's presence and traveled throughout the land.
41.47: During the seven years of abundance the land produced extraordinary harvests.
41.48: Joseph gathered all the food produced throughout those seven years and stored it in the cities—keeping in each city the food from the surrounding fields.
41.49: Joseph stockpiled grain in such enormous quantities—like the sand of the sea—that eventually he stopped keeping records; it was simply beyond measure.
41.50: Before the famine years arrived, two sons were born to Joseph through his wife Asenath, daughter of Potiphera, priest of On.
41.51: Joseph named his firstborn Manasseh—meaning 'God has made me forget'—because God had caused him to forget all his suffering and all his father's household.
41.52: He named his second son Ephraim—meaning 'God has made me fruitful'—because God had made him fruitful in the very land where he had suffered.
41.53: The seven years of abundance in Egypt came to an end,
41.54: and the seven years of famine began—just as Joseph had predicted. Famine struck every surrounding land, but throughout Egypt there was food.
41.55: When the famine spread throughout Egypt and the people cried out to Pharaoh for food, Pharaoh said to all the Egyptians, 'Go to Joseph and do whatever he tells you.'
41.56: As the famine spread over the entire region, Joseph opened the storehouses and sold grain to the Egyptians—for the famine was severe throughout Egypt.
41.57: People from all over the world came to Egypt to buy grain from Joseph, because the famine was severe throughout the earth.

42.1: When Jacob heard there was grain available in Egypt, he said to his sons, 'Why are you all standing around looking at each other?'
42.2: 'I have heard there is grain in Egypt. Go down and buy some for us, so that we can survive and not starve.'
42.3: So ten of Joseph's brothers went down to Egypt to buy grain.
42.4: Jacob did not send Benjamin, Joseph's full brother, with the others. He was afraid something bad might happen to him.
42.5: Israel's sons came as part of the crowds seeking grain, for the famine had struck Canaan as well.
42.6: Now Joseph was the governor of the land—the one who sold grain to everyone who came. When his brothers arrived, they bowed down before him with their faces to the ground.
42.7: Joseph recognized his brothers the moment he saw them. But he pretended to be a stranger and spoke harshly to them. 'Where do you come from?' he demanded. 'From the land of Canaan, to buy food,' they answered.
42.8: Joseph recognized them, but they had no idea who he was.
42.9: Joseph remembered the dreams he had dreamed about them. He said, 'You are spies! You have come to find the weaknesses of our land.'
42.10: 'No, my lord!' they protested. 'Your servants have come only to buy food.'
42.11: 'We are all sons of one man. We are honest—we are not spies.'
42.12: But he insisted, 'No—you have come to find our vulnerabilities.'
42.13: They replied, 'We are twelve brothers, sons of one man in the land of Canaan. The youngest is still with our father, and one of our brothers is gone.'
42.14: Joseph said, 'It is just as I said—you are spies.'
42.15: 'Here is how I will test you: As Pharaoh lives, you will not leave this place unless your youngest brother comes here.'
42.16: 'Send one of you to bring your brother while the rest of you stay in prison. Your story will be put to the test. If you are lying, then—as Pharaoh lives—you are spies!'
42.17: He had all of them locked up for three days.
42.18: On the third day Joseph said to them, 'Do what I say and you will live—I am a man who fears God.'
42.19: 'If you are telling the truth, let one of your brothers stay here in prison while the rest of you go home and bring grain to your starving families.'
42.20: 'But you must bring your youngest brother back to me. That will prove your story, and you won't die.' They agreed to do this.
42.21: They said to each other, 'We are being punished for what we did to our brother. We saw his anguish when he begged us—and we refused to listen. That is why this suffering has come on us.'
42.22: Reuben replied, 'Didn't I tell you not to harm the boy? But you refused to listen! Now we are paying the price for his blood.'
42.23: They did not know that Joseph could understand them, since he had been speaking through an interpreter.
42.24: Joseph turned away from them and wept. Then he turned back, spoke to them again, and had Simeon taken and bound before their eyes.
42.25: Joseph ordered that their sacks be filled with grain and that each man's money be put back in his sack, and that they be given provisions for the journey. These instructions were carried out.
42.26: They loaded their grain onto their donkeys and set out.
42.27: At the place where they stopped for the night, one of them opened his sack to feed his donkey and saw his money right there at the top of the sack.
42.28: 'My money has been returned!' he called to his brothers. 'Here it is in my sack!' Their hearts sank. They looked at each other, trembling. 'What is this that God has done to us?'
42.29: When they arrived back in Canaan, they told their father Jacob everything that had happened.
42.30: 'The man who governs that land spoke harshly to us and accused us of spying.'
42.31: 'We told him, "We are honest men—we are not spies."'
42.32: '"We are twelve brothers, sons of one father. One is dead, and the youngest is still with our father in Canaan."'
42.33: 'Then the man said, "Here is how I will know if you are telling the truth: leave one of your brothers here with me. Take grain for your hungry families and go."'
42.34: '"But bring your youngest brother to me—then I will know you are not spies, and I will return your other brother to you, and you can move freely in this land."'
42.35: As they emptied their sacks, each man found his pouch of money at the top. When they and their father saw the money, they were all frightened.
42.36: Their father Jacob said to them, 'You are robbing me of my children! Joseph is gone, Simeon is gone, and now you want to take Benjamin too. Everything is against me!'
42.37: Reuben said to his father, 'You can put my two sons to death if I fail to bring Benjamin back. Trust him to my care, and I will return him safely.'
42.38: But Jacob said, 'My son will not go down with you. His brother is dead, and he is the only one I have left from Rachel. If anything happened to him on the way, you would send this old man to his grave in grief.'

43.1: The famine remained severe throughout the land.
43.2: When they had eaten all the grain they had brought from Egypt, their father said to them, 'Go back and buy us some more food.'
43.3: But Judah said to him, 'The man gave us a strict warning: "You will not see my face again unless your brother is with you."'
43.4: 'If you are willing to send our brother with us, we will go down and buy food for you.'
43.5: 'But if you won't send him, we won't go—because the man told us clearly, "You will not see my face unless your brother is with you."'
43.6: Israel said, 'Why did you cause me this trouble by telling the man you had another brother?'
43.7: They said, 'He questioned us closely about ourselves and our family—"Is your father still alive? Do you have another brother?" We simply answered what he asked. How could we have known he would say, "Bring your brother here"?'
43.8: Judah said to his father Israel, 'Send the boy with me. Let us get moving so that we—you, us, and our children—can survive and not die.'
43.9: 'I will personally guarantee his safety. If I fail to bring him back and set him before you, I will bear the blame my entire life.'
43.10: 'If we had not wasted all this time, we could have made the trip twice over by now.'
43.11: Their father Israel said to them, 'If that is how it must be, then do this: take some of the finest produce of the land as a gift for the man—some balm, a little honey, spices, myrrh, pistachio nuts, and almonds.'
43.12: 'Also take double the money—bring back the money that was returned in your sacks, in case it was an error.'
43.13: 'Take your brother and go back to the man right away.'
43.14: 'May God Almighty give you favor before the man, so that he releases your other brother and allows Benjamin to come home. As for me—if I lose my children, then I lose them.'
43.15: The men took the gifts, double the money, and Benjamin, and hurried down to Egypt. They presented themselves before Joseph.
43.16: When Joseph saw Benjamin with them, he said to the steward of his house, 'Bring these men inside. Slaughter an animal and prepare a meal—they will dine with me at noon.'
43.17: The steward did as Joseph said and brought the men to Joseph's house.
43.18: The men were terrified to be brought into Joseph's house. They thought, 'It's because of the money returned in our sacks last time. He is going to use it as an excuse to seize us as slaves and take our donkeys.'
43.19: So they approached the steward at the entrance of the house and spoke to him.
43.20: 'Please, sir,' they said, 'we came down before to buy food.'
43.21: 'When we stopped for the night and opened our sacks, each man's money was there in full—right at the top of his sack. We have brought it all back with us.'
43.22: 'And we have brought more money to buy food. We have no idea who put the money in our sacks.'
43.23: 'It's all right—don't be afraid,' the steward said. 'Your God, the God of your father, must have put treasure in your sacks. I received your full payment.' Then he brought Simeon out to them.
43.24: The steward brought them inside, gave them water to wash their feet, and provided fodder for their donkeys.
43.25: They arranged their gifts, ready for when Joseph arrived at noon—for they had been told they would eat a meal there.
43.26: When Joseph came home, they brought their gifts inside and bowed down to him to the ground.
43.27: He greeted them and then asked, 'How is your father—the old man you mentioned? Is he still alive and well?'
43.28: 'Your servant our father is well—he is still alive,' they answered. And they bowed low in respect.
43.29: Joseph looked up and saw his brother Benjamin—his own mother's son. 'Is this your youngest brother, the one you told me about?' he asked. Then he said, 'God be gracious to you, my son.'
43.30: Joseph hurried out of the room—his heart was overwhelmed with love for his brother. He went into his private chamber and wept there.
43.31: After washing his face, he came back out. Composing himself, he ordered, 'Serve the meal.'
43.32: Joseph was served at his own table, the brothers at their own table, and the Egyptians at a separate table—because Egyptians considered it an abomination to eat with Hebrews.
43.33: The brothers were seated before Joseph in order of their ages from oldest to youngest—and they stared at each other in bewilderment.
43.34: Portions were brought to them from Joseph's own table, but Benjamin received five times as much as anyone else. And they all drank freely and were merry with him.

44.1: Joseph gave his steward orders: 'Fill the men's sacks with as much food as they can carry, and put each man's money back in the top of his sack.'
44.2: 'And put my silver cup in the opening of the youngest one's sack, along with his grain money.' The steward did exactly as Joseph instructed.
44.3: At first light, the men were sent off with their donkeys.
44.4: They had barely left the city when Joseph said to his steward, 'Go after those men! When you catch up to them, say, "Why have you repaid good with evil?"'
44.5: 'That is the cup my master drinks from—and uses for divination. What you have done is wicked!'
44.6: The steward caught up with them and confronted them with these words.
44.7: 'How can you say this?' they protested. 'Your servants would never do such a thing!'
44.8: 'We brought back from Canaan every coin we found in our sacks—why on earth would we steal silver or gold from your master's house?'
44.9: 'If any one of us is found with it, let him die—and the rest of us will become your master's slaves.'
44.10: 'Very well,' the steward replied. 'It will be as you say: whoever is found to have it will become my slave—but the rest of you will go free.'
44.11: Each man quickly set his sack on the ground and opened it.
44.12: The steward searched each sack in order, starting with the oldest and working down to the youngest. The cup was found in Benjamin's sack.
44.13: At that, they all tore their clothes in anguish. Each man reloaded his donkey, and they turned back to the city.
44.14: When Judah and his brothers reached Joseph's house, Joseph was still there. They fell to the ground before him.
44.15: 'What have you done?' Joseph demanded. 'Did you not know that a man like me has ways of finding out the truth?'
44.16: Judah said, 'What can we say? What argument can we make? How can we prove our innocence? God himself has exposed the guilt of your servants. We are all your slaves—both those who are innocent and the one found with the cup.'
44.17: But Joseph said, 'I would never do that. Only the man in whose sack the cup was found will be my slave. The rest of you—go back to your father in peace.'
44.18: Then Judah stepped forward and said, 'My lord, please let your servant speak a word in your ear. Do not be angry with me—for you have authority equal to Pharaoh himself.'
44.19: 'My lord asked us, "Do you have a father or a brother?"'
44.20: 'We told you: "We have an elderly father, and a young brother born to him in his old age. His brother is dead—he alone remains of his mother's children, and his father loves him deeply."'
44.21: 'Then you told your servants, "Bring him down to me so I can see him with my own eyes."'
44.22: 'We told you, "The boy cannot leave his father. If he were to leave him, his father would die."'
44.23: 'But you said to us, "Unless your youngest brother comes with you, you will never see my face again."'
44.24: 'When we returned to our father—your servant—we told him everything you had said.'
44.25: 'When our father told us, "Go back and buy some more food,"'
44.26: 'we told him, "We cannot go without our youngest brother. We cannot face that man unless he is with us."'
44.27: 'Then your servant—our father—said to us, "You know that my wife bore me two sons."'
44.28: '"One of them left me, and I said, "He must have been torn to pieces." I have never seen him again.'
44.29: '"If you take this one from me as well and something happens to him, you will send this old man to his grave in utter grief."'
44.30: 'So now, when I return to my father without the boy—and his very life is wrapped up in the boy's life—'
44.31: 'the moment he sees the boy is gone, he will die. Your servants will have sent our father—a broken old man—down to his grave.'
44.32: 'I personally vouched for the boy's safety to my father. I said, "If I do not bring him back, I will bear the blame before you for the rest of my life."'
44.33: 'So please—let me remain here as your slave in place of the boy. Let him go home with his brothers.'
44.34: 'How could I face my father without the boy? I could not bear to see the grief it would bring him.'

45.1: Joseph could hold himself back no longer. With all his attendants present, he cried out, 'Clear the room!' And everyone withdrew. He was alone with his brothers when he revealed who he was.
45.2: He wept so loudly that the Egyptians outside heard it, and word spread through Pharaoh's entire palace.
45.3: Joseph said to his brothers, 'I am Joseph. Is my father still alive?' But his brothers could not say a word—they were too shaken at the sight of him.
45.4: Joseph said, 'Come closer.' They came near. He said, 'I am your brother Joseph—the one you sold into Egypt.'
45.5: 'Do not be grieved or angry with yourselves for what you did. God sent me ahead of you to save lives.'
45.6: 'The famine has been in the land for two years now, and five more years are coming with no plowing or harvest.'
45.7: 'God sent me ahead of you to preserve a remnant for you in the land and to keep you alive through a great rescue.'
45.8: 'So it was not you who sent me here—it was God. He has made me a chief counselor to Pharaoh, lord of his whole household, and ruler over all of Egypt.'
45.9: 'Hurry back to my father and tell him: "Your son Joseph says this—God has made me lord of all Egypt. Come down to me right away; do not delay."'
45.10: 'You will settle in the land of Goshen, close to me—you and your children and grandchildren, your flocks, your herds, everything you own.'
45.11: 'I will take care of you there, because five years of famine still remain. I do not want you and your household—everyone belonging to you—to be reduced to poverty.'
45.12: 'You can see with your own eyes—and Benjamin can see—that it is truly I, Joseph, speaking to you.'
45.13: 'Tell my father about all my honor here in Egypt, everything you have seen. And bring him here to me—quickly!'
45.14: He threw his arms around his brother Benjamin and wept. Benjamin wept on his neck.
45.15: He kissed each of his brothers and wept over them. After that, his brothers were able to speak with him.
45.16: When word spread through Pharaoh's palace that Joseph's brothers had arrived, Pharaoh and all his officials were delighted.
45.17: Pharaoh said to Joseph, 'Tell your brothers: "Do this—load up your animals and return to Canaan."'
45.18: '"Bring your father and your families back to me. I will give you the finest land in Egypt, and you will enjoy all its richness."'
45.19: 'Also tell them: "Take wagons from Egypt for your wives and your children. Bring your father and come."'
45.20: '"Do not worry about your possessions—the finest of all Egypt will be waiting for you."'
45.21: Israel's sons did as they were told. Joseph gave them wagons as Pharaoh had ordered, and he provided them with supplies for the road.
45.22: He gave each brother a fresh set of clothing, but to Benjamin he gave three hundred pieces of silver and five sets of clothing.
45.23: For his father he sent ten donkeys loaded with the finest things from Egypt, and ten female donkeys carrying grain, bread, and provisions for the journey.
45.24: He sent his brothers on their way. As they left, he told them, 'Don't argue with each other along the way!'
45.25: So they went up from Egypt and arrived in the land of Canaan, back to their father Jacob.
45.26: 'Joseph is still alive!' they told him. 'In fact, he is ruler over all of Egypt.' Jacob's heart went numb—he could not believe it.
45.27: But when they reported everything Joseph had said, and when Jacob saw the wagons Joseph had sent to bring him—his spirit came back to life.
45.28: Israel said, 'Enough! My son Joseph is alive! I will go and see him before I die.'

46.1: Israel set out with everything he owned and came to Beersheba, where he offered sacrifices to the God of his father Isaac.
46.2: God spoke to Israel in a vision in the night: 'Jacob! Jacob!' 'Here I am,' he answered.
46.3: 'I am God—the God of your father. Do not be afraid to go down to Egypt. There I will make you into a great nation.'
46.4: 'I will go down to Egypt with you, and I will surely bring you back again. Joseph himself will close your eyes at the end.'
46.5: Jacob left Beersheba, and his sons brought their father Jacob, their children, and their wives in the wagons Pharaoh had sent.
46.6: They also brought their livestock and everything they had accumulated in Canaan. Jacob and all his descendants went to Egypt.
46.7: He brought his sons and grandsons, his daughters and granddaughters—all his descendants—to Egypt.
46.8: These are the names of Jacob's descendants who went to Egypt: Reuben, Jacob's firstborn;
46.9: the sons of Reuben: Hanoch, Pallu, Hezron, and Carmi.
46.10: the sons of Simeon: Jemuel, Jamin, Ohad, Jachin, Zohar, and Shaul the son of a Canaanite woman.
46.11: the sons of Levi: Gershon, Kohath, and Merari.
46.12: the sons of Judah: Er, Onan, Shelah, Perez, and Zerah—though Er and Onan had died in Canaan. The sons of Perez: Hezron and Hamul.
46.13: the sons of Issachar: Tola, Puah, Jashub, and Shimron.
46.14: the sons of Zebulun: Sered, Elon, and Jahleel.
46.15: (These were the children Leah bore to Jacob in Paddan-aram, along with his daughter Dinah—thirty-three in all.)
46.16: the sons of Gad: Zephon, Haggi, Shuni, Ezbon, Eri, Arodi, and Areli.
46.17: the sons of Asher: Imnah, Ishvah, Ishvi, and Beriah, and their sister Serah. The sons of Beriah: Heber and Malchiel.
46.18: (These were the children born to Jacob through Zilpah, whom Laban gave to his daughter Leah—sixteen in all.)
46.19: the sons of Jacob's wife Rachel: Joseph and Benjamin.
46.20: In Egypt, Joseph's wife Asenath—daughter of Potiphera, priest of On—bore him Manasseh and Ephraim.
46.21: the sons of Benjamin: Bela, Beker, Ashbel, Gera, Naaman, Ehi, Rosh, Muppim, Huppim, and Ard.
46.22: (These were the children born to Jacob through Rachel—fourteen in all.)
46.24: the sons of Naphtali: Jahzeel, Guni, Jezer, and Shillem.
46.25: (These were the children born to Jacob through Bilhah, whom Laban gave to his daughter Rachel—seven in all.)
46.26: All the people who went to Egypt with Jacob—his own direct descendants, not counting the wives of his sons—numbered sixty-six.
46.27: Including the two sons born to Joseph in Egypt, the total of Jacob's household who came to Egypt was seventy.
46.28: Jacob had sent Judah ahead to Joseph to prepare the way to Goshen. They arrived in the land of Goshen.
46.29: Joseph had his chariot readied and went up to Goshen to meet his father Israel. He fell on his father's neck and wept there for a long time.
46.30: Israel said to Joseph, 'Now I can die content—I have seen your face and know you are alive.'
46.31: Joseph said to his brothers and to his father's household, 'I am going to speak to Pharaoh and tell him: "My brothers and my father's household from Canaan have come to me."'
46.32: 'These men are shepherds who have always herded livestock. They have brought their flocks, their herds, and all their possessions.'
46.33: 'When Pharaoh summons you and asks what your occupation is,'
46.34: 'tell him: "Your servants have been herdsmen from our youth until now—as our fathers were before us." This way you will be given the land of Goshen to live in, since Egyptians consider all shepherds an abomination.'

47.1: Joseph went to Pharaoh and said, 'My father and brothers have arrived from Canaan with their flocks, their herds, and all they own. They are now in the land of Goshen.'
47.2: He selected five of his brothers and brought them before Pharaoh.
47.3: Pharaoh asked the brothers, 'What is your occupation?' They answered, 'Your servants are shepherds, as our fathers were before us.'
47.4: They also said, 'We have come to live here for a time, because the famine is severe in Canaan and there is no grazing for our flocks. Please allow your servants to settle in the land of Goshen.'
47.5: Pharaoh said to Joseph, 'Your father and brothers have come to you.'
47.6: 'The land of Egypt is open to you. Settle your father and brothers in the best part of the land—let them live in Goshen. And if any of them are particularly capable, put them in charge of my own herds.'
47.7: Then Joseph brought his father Jacob in and presented him to Pharaoh. Jacob blessed Pharaoh.
47.8: Pharaoh asked him, 'How old are you?'
47.9: Jacob said to Pharaoh, 'The years of my pilgrimage are one hundred and thirty—few in number and filled with hardship. I have not reached the years my fathers lived during their sojourning.'
47.10: Then Jacob blessed Pharaoh and withdrew from his presence.
47.11: Joseph settled his father and brothers and gave them a holding in the finest part of Egypt—the district of Rameses—exactly as Pharaoh had commanded.
47.12: Joseph provided food for his father, his brothers, and everyone in his father's household, according to how many children each family had.
47.13: There was no food anywhere in the region. The famine was so severe that both Egypt and Canaan were wasting away.
47.14: Joseph collected all the silver in Egypt and Canaan in exchange for grain, and he brought it all into Pharaoh's treasury.
47.15: When all the money in Egypt and Canaan was gone, all the Egyptians came to Joseph. 'Give us food!' they said. 'Why should we die right in front of you? Our money is gone.'
47.16: Joseph said, 'If your money is gone, bring your livestock. I will accept your animals in exchange for food.'
47.17: So they brought their livestock—horses, sheep, cattle, donkeys—and Joseph gave them food in return. He got them through that year by accepting all their animals in exchange for grain.
47.18: When that year ended, they came back the next year. 'We will not hide this from you, my lord,' they said. 'Our money is gone. Our livestock belongs to you. All we have left is our bodies and our land.'
47.19: 'Why should we die before your eyes—us and our land both? Buy us and our land in exchange for food, and we and our land will become Pharaoh's. Just give us seed so we can live and not die—so the land does not return to wasteland.'
47.20: So Joseph bought up all the land in Egypt for Pharaoh. The Egyptians sold their fields one by one—the famine was too severe. All the land became Pharaoh's.
47.21: And Joseph reduced the people to servants throughout the length of Egypt.
47.22: The only land he did not buy was the priestly land, because the priests received a regular allotment from Pharaoh and had enough to eat from what Pharaoh gave them. So they did not need to sell their land.
47.23: Joseph said to the people, 'Today I have purchased you and your land for Pharaoh. Here is seed for you—plant the land.'
47.24: 'At harvest time, give one-fifth to Pharaoh. The remaining four-fifths are yours—for seed, for yourselves, your households, and your children.'
47.25: 'You have saved our lives!' they said. 'May we find favor with you, my lord—we will serve Pharaoh.'
47.26: So Joseph established a law throughout Egypt—which stands to this day—that one-fifth of all produce belongs to Pharaoh. Only the priestly land was never transferred to Pharaoh.
47.27: So Israel settled in the land of Egypt—in the land of Goshen. They acquired property there, were fruitful, and grew in number greatly.
47.28: Jacob lived in Egypt for seventeen years, so that the full span of his life was one hundred and forty-seven years.
47.29: When the time drew near for Israel to die, he called for his son Joseph and said, 'If I have found favor with you, put your hand under my thigh and swear to show me faithful covenant loyalty and truth. Do not bury me in Egypt.'
47.30: 'When I sleep with my fathers, carry me out of Egypt and bury me in their burial place.' Joseph answered, 'I will do what you ask.'
47.31: 'Swear it to me,' Jacob said. Joseph swore. And Israel bowed in worship at the head of his bed.

48.1: Some time later, Joseph received word: 'Your father is ill.' He took his two sons Manasseh and Ephraim with him.
48.2: When Jacob was told, 'Your son Joseph has come,' Israel gathered his strength and sat up in bed.
48.3: Jacob said to Joseph, 'God Almighty appeared to me at Luz in Canaan and blessed me.'
48.4: 'He said to me, "I will make you fruitful and multiply your numbers. I will make you into a community of peoples, and I will give this land to your descendants as a permanent possession."'
48.5: 'Now your two sons born to you in Egypt before I came here—Ephraim and Manasseh—are mine. They are counted as my sons, just as Reuben and Simeon are mine.'
48.6: 'Any children you have after them will be yours. But in matters of inheritance they will be counted under the names of their two brothers.'
48.7: 'As I was coming from Paddan, Rachel died—and it was a grief to me—in the land of Canaan, while we were on the road, still some distance from Ephrath. I buried her there, right by the road to Ephrath, which is Bethlehem.'
48.8: Israel noticed Joseph's sons and asked, 'Who are these boys?'
48.9: 'These are my sons, whom God has given me here in Egypt,' Joseph said. Israel said, 'Bring them to me so I can bless them.'
48.10: Israel's eyes were dim with age and he could barely see. Joseph brought the boys close, and Jacob kissed them and held them.
48.11: Israel said to Joseph, 'I never thought I would see your face again—and now God has even let me see your children.'
48.12: Joseph drew them away from Jacob's knees and bowed down to the ground before him.
48.13: Joseph took both boys—Ephraim on his right, positioned toward Israel's left, and Manasseh on his left, toward Israel's right—and brought them close.
48.14: But Israel deliberately crossed his arms—placing his right hand on Ephraim's head, the younger one, and his left hand on Manasseh's head, even though Manasseh was the firstborn.
48.15: He blessed Joseph and said: 'May the God before whom my fathers Abraham and Isaac walked— the God who has been my shepherd all my life long to this very day—'
48.16: 'and the Angel who has redeemed me from every harm— bless these boys. May my name live on in them, and the name of my fathers Abraham and Isaac. May they grow into a great multitude throughout the earth.'
48.17: When Joseph saw that his father had placed his right hand on Ephraim's head, he was displeased. He reached out to move his father's hand from Ephraim's head to Manasseh's.
48.18: 'No, Father,' Joseph said. 'This one is the firstborn. Put your right hand on his head.'
48.19: But his father refused. 'I know, my son, I know,' he said. 'Manasseh will also become a great people. But his younger brother will be greater—his descendants will become a multitude of nations.'
48.20: He blessed them that day with these words: 'In your names, Israel will give blessings, saying, "May God make you like Ephraim and Manasseh."' In this way he placed Ephraim before Manasseh.
48.21: Then Israel said to Joseph, 'I am about to die. But God will be with you and will bring you back to the land of your fathers.'
48.22: 'And to you above your brothers I give the ridge of land—Shechem—that I took from the Amorites with my sword and bow.'

49.1: Jacob called for his sons. 'Come together,' he said, 'so I may tell you what lies ahead in the days to come.'
49.2: 'Gather and hear, O sons of Jacob; listen to Israel your father.'
49.3: 'Reuben, my firstborn— my strength, the firstfruits of my vigor; first in honor, first in power.'
49.4: 'Reckless as floodwaters, you shall not have preeminence— for you climbed into your father's bed and defiled his couch. You went up!'
49.5: 'Simeon and Levi are brothers— their very weapons are instruments of brutality.'
49.6: 'Let me have no part in their council; let my honor not be joined to their company— for in their rage they murdered men, and in their recklessness they hamstrung oxen.'
49.7: 'Cursed be their ferocious anger, their brutal fury! I will scatter them throughout Jacob and disperse them across Israel.'
49.8: 'Judah—your brothers will praise you; your hand will grip the necks of your enemies; your father's sons will bow before you.'
49.9: 'Judah is a lion's cub— he climbs back from the kill, my son. He crouches, he lies down like a lion, like a great lioness— who would dare rouse him?'
49.10: 'The scepter will not leave Judah, nor the ruler's staff from between his feet— until he comes to whom kingship belongs; and the peoples will gather to obey him.'
49.11: 'He ties his donkey to the vine, his colt to the finest branch— he washes his robes in wine, his garments in the juice of grapes.'
49.12: 'His eyes are darker than wine, his teeth brighter than milk.'
49.13: 'Zebulun will dwell at the seashore, a harbor for ships, with his border reaching toward Sidon.'
49.14: 'Issachar is a sturdy donkey crouching between two loaded panniers.'
49.15: 'He saw the resting place was good and the land was pleasant— so he bent his back to the load and became a laborer under tribute.'
49.16: 'Dan will judge his people like any other tribe in Israel.'
49.17: 'Dan will be a serpent on the road, a viper on the path— striking the horse's heel so its rider falls backward.'
49.18: 'LORD, I look to you for salvation!'
49.19: 'Gad—raiders will raid him, but he will raid at their heels.'
49.20: 'Asher will eat from rich soil and furnish delicacies worthy of a king's table.'
49.21: 'Naphtali is a swift doe released to the open— she bears beautiful fawns.'
49.22: 'Joseph is a fruitful vine growing beside a spring— his branches climb and spread over the wall.'
49.23: 'Archers attacked him with bitterness, they shot at him and pressed him hard—'
49.24: 'yet his bow held steady and his arms stayed strong— by the hands of the Mighty One of Jacob, by the Shepherd and Stone of Israel—'
49.25: 'by your father's God who helps you, by the Almighty who blesses you— with blessings from the heavens above, blessings from the deep below, blessings of the breast and the womb.'
49.26: 'The blessings of your father surpass those of the ancient mountains, the gifts of the eternal hills— may they rest on the head of Joseph, on the brow of the one set apart from his brothers.'
49.27: 'Benjamin is a ravenous wolf— in the morning he devours his prey, at evening he divides the spoil.'
49.28: These are the twelve tribes of Israel. This is what their father spoke over them when he blessed them, giving to each the blessing that fit him.
49.29: Then he gave them their final instructions: 'I am about to be gathered to my people. Bury me with my fathers in the cave in the field of Ephron the Hittite—'
49.30: 'the cave at Machpelah, east of Mamre in Canaan—the cave Abraham bought from Ephron the Hittite as a burial ground.'
49.31: 'Abraham and Sarah are buried there, Isaac and Rebekah are buried there, and there I buried Leah.'
49.32: 'The field and the cave were purchased from the Hittites.'
49.33: When Jacob had finished his final words to his sons, he drew his feet up into the bed, breathed his last breath, and was gathered to his people.

50.1: Joseph fell on his father's face, wept over him, and kissed him.
50.2: Joseph ordered the physicians who served him to embalm his father. They embalmed Israel.
50.3: The embalming took the full forty days required. And the Egyptians mourned him for seventy days.
50.4: When the mourning period was over, Joseph spoke to Pharaoh's household: 'If I have found favor with you, please speak to Pharaoh on my behalf. Tell him:'
50.5: '"My father made me swear: he said, "I am about to die—bury me in the tomb I cut for myself in Canaan." Please allow me to go and bury my father, and then I will return."'
50.6: Pharaoh answered, 'Go and bury your father, as you swore to him.'
50.7: So Joseph went up to bury his father. All of Pharaoh's officials accompanied him—the elders of his palace and all the senior officials of Egypt—
50.8: along with all of Joseph's household, his brothers, and his father's household. Only their children, their flocks, and their herds were left behind in Goshen.
50.9: Chariots and cavalry went with them as well. It was an enormous company.
50.10: When they came to the threshing floor of Atad, beyond the Jordan, they mourned with a great and bitter lamentation. Joseph observed a seven-day mourning period for his father there.
50.11: When the Canaanites who lived there saw the mourning at the threshing floor of Atad, they said, 'What solemn mourning by the Egyptians!' So the place was named Abel-mizraim—it is beyond the Jordan.
50.12: Jacob's sons did exactly as he had instructed them.
50.13: They carried him to Canaan and buried him in the cave at Machpelah east of Mamre—the cave Abraham had bought from Ephron the Hittite as a burial ground.
50.14: After burying his father, Joseph returned to Egypt with his brothers and all who had come up with him.
50.15: When Joseph's brothers saw that their father was dead, they said, 'What if Joseph is holding a grudge and decides to pay us back for everything we did to him?'
50.16: They sent a message to Joseph: 'Before your father died, he gave this instruction:'
50.17: '"Tell Joseph: please forgive the transgression of your brothers and the sin they committed against you." Now we also ask you to forgive the sin of the servants of the God of your father.' When this reached Joseph, he wept.
50.18: His brothers came and fell down before him. 'We are your servants,' they said.
50.19: But Joseph said to them, 'Do not be afraid. Am I in the place of God?'
50.20: 'You plotted evil against me—but God wove those very plans into good, so that many people would be kept alive. And so it is today.'
50.21: 'So do not be afraid. I will provide for you and your children.' He comforted them and spoke tenderly to them.
50.22: Joseph remained in Egypt, he and all his father's household. Joseph lived one hundred and ten years.
50.23: He lived to see Ephraim's children to the third generation. The children of Makir, the son of Manasseh, were also counted as his own.
50.24: Joseph said to his brothers, 'I am about to die. But God will surely come to your aid and bring you up out of this land to the land he swore to give Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob.'
50.25: Joseph made the sons of Israel swear an oath: 'God will surely come to your aid—when he does, you must carry my bones up from this place.'
50.26: Joseph died at the age of one hundred and ten. They embalmed him, and he was placed in a coffin in Egypt.
